behaviors.description: A plugin whose manifest is missing a required field is rejected before any code from that plugin is executed.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that plugin code runs despite invalid manifest. Mocking file I/O misses timing bugs where the loader accidentally imports the plugin before validating the manifest. We need real filesystem I/O (to read actual manifest files) plus verification that the plugin module doesn't execute. This could be done by creating a real plugin with observable side effects (file write, global variable set) and proving those don't occur. Unit tests with all mocks can hide import-timing bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test plugin directory with a manifest.json missing a required field and a plugin module that sets a global flag on import. Call the loader and verify: (1) an error is thrown, (2) the global flag is never set (proving module wasn't imported). Use real filesystem for manifests; spy on the flag to verify non-execution.

behaviors.description: When plugin A declares a dependency on plugin B, plugin B's onLoad hook is called before plugin A's.
behaviors.justification: This is testing deterministic dependency resolution and hook call ordering. The logic is pure: topological sort → iterate in order → invoke hooks. All I/O can be mocked (manifest loading) and all plugins can be mocked (create mock objects with spyable onLoad methods). The failure (wrong call order) is detectable by spying on function calls. No filesystem, subprocess, or real imports needed—the algorithm itself is what's being tested.
behaviors.test_description: Create two mock plugins A and B where A depends on B. Mock their onLoad hooks with jest.fn(). Add them to the loader and call load(). Assert that B's onLoad was called before A's by checking call order in the mock call stack.

behaviors.description: When two plugins each declare a dependency on the other, the loader reports the cycle and loads neither.
behaviors.justification: Cycle detection is deterministic, in-process graph logic. A mocked scenario with two plugins declaring circular dependencies is sufficient to test the algorithm: the loader should detect the cycle and throw an error before calling any onLoad hooks. All can be mocked; no I/O or external behavior involved. The failure (missing cycle detection or loading a cycled plugin) is purely algorithmic.
behaviors.test_description: Create two mock plugins A and B, each declaring a dependency on the other. Call loader.load() and assert it throws an error mentioning circular dependency. Verify neither mock's onLoad was called using jest.fn() spy assertions.

behaviors.description: A plugin's onLoad hook is only invoked after the plugin's entry module has been imported and executed.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that onLoad gets called before or without the module's top-level code executing. Mocking the plugin module doesn't verify that its real top-level code actually ran. To prove module execution happens first, we need (a) real plugin files on the filesystem, (b) observable proof that top-level code ran (e.g., a global variable set, a file written), and (c) verification that this proof occurs before onLoad is invoked. Unit tests with all mocks can hide sequencing bugs in the loader's import-then-call logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real plugin file in a test directory with top-level code that sets global.pluginImported = true, and export a spyable onLoad function. Call loader.load() and assert: (1) global.pluginImported is true, (2) onLoad was called after the module loaded (or use a sequential spy to verify import-then-onLoad order). Verify with real filesystem reads and real module imports.

behaviors.description: When a plugin throws an error during its onLoad hook, the loader continues initializing the remaining plugins that do not depend on it.
behaviors.justification: This is testing error handling and continuation logic, which is deterministic and in-process. Create three mock plugins: A (throws on onLoad), B (no dependency on A), and C (depends on A). The failure (loader stops or loads C) is detectable by checking which onLoad hooks were called. All can be mocked; no filesystem or real imports needed. The behavior is purely about how the loader handles exceptions in the plugin execution phase.
behaviors.test_description: Create three mock plugins: A (onLoad throws), B (independent), C (depends on A). Call loader.load(). Assert B's onLoad was called (loader continues with independent plugins). Assert C's onLoad was not called (dependents of failed plugins don't load). Use jest.fn() spies to verify call presence/absence.
